It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! UP moved Green 2 from (30-32, 51-53) to (27-29, 51-53). Green 1 stayed at (30-32, 21-23).
So UP moves the topmost green (or the green in the right panel?). Green 2 was at row 30-32 and moved to 27-29. Green 1 stayed at 30-32.
Wait, both greens were at rows 30-32 before UP. After UP, Green 2 is at 27-29 and Green 1 is at 30-32. So UP moved Green 2 up by 3 rows.
This means UP/DOWN can move greens vertically. Let me check if I can move Green 2 up to align with the right black square.
The right black square is at around row 25-27. Green 2 is now at 27-29. If I move Green 2 up more, it might align with the black square.
But wait, the black square is at row 25-27, and Green 2 is at 27-29. They overlap at row 27. Maybe Green 2 needs to be at the same row as the black square.
Actually, looking at the image, the right black square is higher than Green 2. Let me try UP again to move Green 2 up more.
But actually, I should think about what the goal is. Maybe the goal is to get Green 2 (W-dotted) to align with the right black square (which has a W interior). If they match, maybe the level completes.
Let me try UP again to move Green 2 up to align with the black square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green 1: (30-32, 21-23), dot='c', 8 pixels (left panel)
# Green 2: (27-29, 51-53), dot='W', 8 pixels (right panel, moved up)
# Try UP again to move Green 2 up to align with black square

result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Last action: {last_action_result}")
print("After UP:")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        r_min, c_min = n['boundary'][0]
        r_max, c_max = n['boundary'][2]
        center_r = (r_min + r_max) // 2
        center_c = (c_min + c_max) // 2
        dot_char = ascii_lines[center_r][center_c]
        print(f"  Green at ({r_min}-{r_max}, {c_min}-{c_max}), center=({center_r},{center_c})={dot_char}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
    if n['color'] == 'B':
        r_min, c_min = n['boundary'][0]
        r_max, c_max = n['boundary'][2]
        print(f"  Black at ({r_min}-{r_max}, {c_min}-{c_max})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
